author: @floridacoder
text:
Have you tried mixing cell sizes?  What I'm thinking is one or two very large storage cells - possibly two to overcome the .8 volt drop in a diode.   The storage cell or cells would be in parallel with 6 smaller super capacitors in series and charged by the storage cell(s) with blocking diodes, so we still get 12+ volts from the battery.  Instead of charging with a 12 voltage source you would charge with a 2(ish) volt source.  Your battery would need 3 posts.   1 negative, 1 positive input at ~2 volts.  And 1 positive 12 volt output.  The PV cells would instead be connected all in parallel instead of series for charging. 

Would there be any significant disadvantages to that approach?  What I was thinking was one massive 1 or 2 cell storage battery.  Because of the simplicity it would be extremely easy to manufacture.   That would be the primary power supply for super capacitors.

author: @stevecummins324
text:
a big one for energy storage is the upper limit of breakdown voltage
capacitor energy
E=C *V*V/2
can be expanded with inclusion of what you derived there.... 
E= u0*A*V*V/2*d
if everything else kept same, with double the maximum voltage that a capacitor can hold, the energy storage goes up by *2*2 ie *4

author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: Something for your mind to toy with: If you change your plates into bifilar coils and make your separator into a bifilar coil, will charging the coil plates cause a current flow in the coil separator? The electromagnetic maths say yes. For every change in an electric field there is a change in magnetic and vis-vers-a.

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
Striving for the "highest value possible" has probably gotten us into the problems we have today of needing rare/toxic elements only found in certain locations around the globe.

Going along with things you've been saying on your channel, maybe we should be focusing on "lowest value necessary" that meets our requirements (the big ones being available space, cost of materials, and the interface requirements).  Then, instead of having one super solution to the energy storage problem, we could have a plethora of solutions.

The problem then moves from finding "The Best" energy storage materials to dinging the most accommodating interface that allows you to connect power generation to power usage.  Kind of like the way a AA alkaline battery and an AA lithium battery can fit into the same holder in a flashlight and be able to power the flashlight without any (or very little) modifications.  Then you could take any power storage or production device, and as long as it could connect to the--let's call it the "in" side of the interface, it could power any device that hooks up to the "out" side.

author: @DeeP_BosE
text: Have been using 3000F * 6, 2.8V supercaps in series for my car for the last couple of years. These are just amazing devices. However i find one of the cells(of the 6) leaking a white electrolyte that solidifies and crystalizes in air. This cell also has a very high self discharge rate, which is making me lose balanced condition( i dont use a balancing circuit cz the other 5 have equal capacitance n discharge rate). There is an electrolyte filler screw , i was wondering what i can use, to fill  it up with to temporarily reduce the self discharge. Im guessing oil based electrolyte.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: chances not mate - usually supercpapacitors have an acetonitrile solvent

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering thank you for looking into it.

author: @paulwright5440
text: Why do capacitors loose their energy quicker than batteries when there similar in construction

replies.author: @spamhead
replies.text: I suppose it is because a battery will hold a fairly constant voltage, and therefore power output until it is nearly flat. A capacitor’s voltage starts dropping the second it starts to discharge, and the power available drops proportional the voltage at any given current. If there is a fixed resistive load, the power will drop proportional to the square of the voltage. Presumably, Formula 1 KERS devices have some very fancy electronics to mitigate these problems!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: they don't lose their energy quicker mate - they just don't store a lot

author: @davidabineri908
text: Saying, for example, that capacitance goes up when permittivity goes up is NOT even an intuitive derivation. Please don't believe that there was much of any kind of derivation here.  In earlier videos, this person gets very excited about being able generate a voltage but has not shown it being used for anything. Creating an open circuit voltage is one thing, using it to drive a load (light bulb for example) is a very different proposition.  This seems all to be phony stuff, be careful what you believe.

replies.text: you don't have to believe it - it is something you can test for yourself - stick some different materials between two bits of kitchen foil and charge it and see how long it lights a light - I get a sense you have never even tried that - or am I wrong there?
